text: The surgeon uses a bougie tube to decompress the stomach, revealing the entrance of the esophagus and stomach. Adhesions from the diaphragm are being dissected to evaluate the anatomy for a hiatal hernia. The spleen is visible on the top right, and a retractor lifts a portion of the liver on the top left. After dissecting the diaphragm and esophagus, no hiatal hernia is observed, allowing the procedure to proceed.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to cauterize and cut tissue, preventing bleeding. The size of the gastric pouch is measured to be approximately two inches. A tunnel is created underneath the stomach to align the staplers. The stapler is placed across the stomach, and after confirming the pouch size, it is fired. The tunnel extends to the top of the stomach near the esophagus, and the staplers divide the tissue, completely separating the pouch from the gastric remnant.

The surgeon uses a suture to connect the corner of the gastric pouch to the small intestine, creating the gastrojejunostomy anastomosis. This connection forms the first portion of the Roux limb, with the suture visibly passing through the gastric pouch and jejunum.

The surgeon uses scissors to create a gastrostomy, an opening in the gastric pouch. A plastic bougie tube is visible, aiding in measuring the pouch size. Subsequently, another opening is made in the small intestine, preparing for the anastomosis between the pouch and intestine.

The surgeon completes the second layer of suturing to reinforce the anastomosis, preventing leaks. The final view shows the roux limb of the small intestine. The surgeon then divides the intestine to create the biliopancreatic limb, visible on the right side of the screen, which will deliver pancreatic enzymes and bile to the roux limb for digestion. The roux limb measures 150 centimeters and will initially carry undigested food until it connects with the biliopancreatic limb.

The surgeon creates a second anastomosis by making an opening in both the roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb. These openings are then stapled together to form a jejuno-jejunostomy, allowing bile and pancreatic enzymes to mix with food in the roux limb, initiating digestion.

The surgeon sutures the common enterotomy to close the opening, then uses a stapler to seal the jejuno-jejunostomy (JJ) connection. The potential hernia space created by the connection of the two small intestines is closed with permanent sutures to prevent future complications. The Peterson's defect, another potential space for internal hernias, is addressed by placing sutures in the mesentery of the roux limb and the colon, which is lifted to the left of the screen.

The surgeon performs a leak test by filling the abdomen with saline and instilling air into the connection, observing for air bubbles in the saline. No bubbles are seen, indicating no leaks. The bougie is removed from the gastric pouch, and the final view shows the roux limb of the small intestine with the jejuno-jejunostomy visible on the right side of the screen.
